one_line_takeaway: PASA moves LLM watermarking from token-level to semantic-cluster-level shared randomness, and under this paper's own experiments it substantially outperforms KGW, Exp-edit, AWTI, and DAWA on detection robustness against T5-based token replacement and DIPPER paraphrasing at low FPR, while remaining close to distortion-free in perplexity and adding negligible generation/detection latency.
key_finding: On clean text PASA achieves near-perfect detection (ROC-AUC/TPR at or near 1.0) on both Llama-2-13B and Mixtral-8x7B, matching the best baselines. Under T5-Large token-replacement attack on Llama-2-13B, PASA attains TPR@1%FPR of 0.9296 versus KGW's 0.7350 and DAWA's 0.3300; under the stronger T5-XXL attack PASA's AUROC is 0.9392, exceeding KGW (0.9196) and roughly matching Exp-edit (0.9536). On Mixtral-8x7B under T5-Large attack, PASA reaches AUROC 0.9902, surpassing Exp-edit (0.9760). Under DIPPER paraphrasing, PASA is the most robust across all three settings: at Ord=0/20/80 it attains TPR@1%FPR of 0.5900/0.5693/0.5377 with AUROC of 0.9132/0.8993/0.8956 (note the paper's prose in Sec 4.2 reports slightly different rounded figures -- 0.5578/0.5829/0.5879 TPR and 0.8776/0.9116/0.8934 AUROC -- for the same rows, an internal inconsistency between text and Table 2 that should be flagged rather than silently reconciled), while token-level baselines collapse: at Ord=80, DAWA's TPR@1%FPR drops to 0.0200 and KGW's to 0.3100 (text cites 0.3050), and even robust-editing baselines Exp-edit and AWTI fall to 0.1150 and 0.1350 respectively. On text quality (Table 3), PASA's PPL is 11.44, close to unwatermarked (12.41) and human text (10.41), and better than Exp-edit (23.40) and AWTI (19.77), though DAWA achieves a lower PPL (8.41). PASA's generation latency (13.35s per 300-token sample) is close to the unwatermarked baseline (12.93s) and comparable to DAWA (13.56s), far below AWTI (24.24s); detection latency (0.27s) is the lowest among robust methods, beating Exp-edit (2.41s) and AWTI (10.52s), though slower than KGW's simple counting detector (0.04s). Ablations show K=4 is near-optimal: clean-text detection is near-perfect for all K, robustness against T5-based attacks holds well for K in [3,100], but degrades sharply once K exceeds ~500 (theoretically predicted by Theorem 1's K-dependence, and empirically compounded by seed-recovery fragility). Larger synchronization window w hurts robustness: under r=0.5 token replacement, TPR@1%FPR falls from 0.7236 at w=3 to 0.1508 at w=8.
core_contribution: PASA (Principled watermarking Approach under Semantic-invariant Attacks) is a distortion-free LLM text watermarking scheme that embeds and detects the watermark at the level of semantic clusters in a latent token-embedding space rather than at the token level. It partitions the token embedding space (via K-means on embeddings from gte-Qwen2-7B-instruct) into K disjoint semantic clusters, and synchronizes watermark 'shared randomness' using a PRF seeded by the secret key plus the semantic-cluster history of the previous w tokens (rather than raw token history as in prior schemes like KGW). Watermark embedding uses a two-stage sampling strategy: (1) sample an auxiliary variable zeta over clusters (with an 'overflow state' to control false-alarm rate), then (2) sample the next token within the selected cluster via a re-normalized distribution that provably preserves the original next-token-prediction distribution in expectation (distortion-free). Detection uses a lightweight surrogate LM (SLM) to replay the same process and accumulate an indicator score of cluster-vs-resampled-zeta matches. The paper also derives a theoretical framework (extending He et al. 2025's DAWA framework) that formalizes semantic-invariant attacks as arbitrary token substitutions within an attacker-controlled equivalence class induced by a semantic mapping function f, states a minimum miss-detection-error theorem (Theorem 1) showing the accuracy/robustness/distortion trade-off is governed by cluster count K, and characterizes (Theorem 2, informal) the jointly optimal embedding-detection pair under this attack model, which PASA's two-stage sampling instantiates.
threat_model: Detector is a defender trying to attribute LLM-generated text; the adversary is anyone attempting to evade watermark detection via 'semantic-invariant attacks' -- meaning-preserving text transformations such as synonym/token substitution or full paraphrasing that leave semantics intact but can arbitrarily change token realizations. Formally, the adversary can modify any token sequence x^T to any other sequence within the same semantic equivalence class B_f(x^T) induced by the semantic mapping function f. The paper evaluates worst-case false-alarm error (human text wrongly flagged) and worst-case miss-detection error (watermarked-then-attacked text wrongly passed as human) under this attack class. Concretely instantiated attacks in experiments are (i) contextual token replacement via T5-Large/T5-XXL infilling at mask ratios 0.3/0.5, and (ii) DIPPER paraphrasing at three lexical/order-diversity settings (Lex=60, Ord=0/20/80). The detector assumes access to a secret key, the semantic mapping function f, the FA constraint alpha, and a surrogate LM (SLM) approximating the generator's NTP distribution (black-box, text-only detection -- no access to the original model's logits at detection time). No adaptive/white-box attacker against the watermarking algorithm itself (e.g., an attacker who knows f and the clustering and specifically targets it) is evaluated; robustness is only tested against generic paraphrasing/substitution tools, not watermark-aware adversaries.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 6): (1) PASA may still degrade under very strong rewriting/watermark-removal attacks that substantially alter both semantic content and distributional structure, not just surface form; (2) detection on very short texts is weak because the detector aggregates token-level statistical evidence and short sequences yield too few observations; (3) PASA depends on the detector-side SLM being tokenizer-compatible with the generator model -- a tokenizer mismatch degrades embedding/detection consistency and cross-model-family transferability, for which the authors suggest (but do not evaluate) deploying multiple candidate-family SLM detectors. Additional grounding-based observations: Theorem 2 is explicitly presented as 'informal' in the main text (full formal statement/proof deferred to Appendix D, not reviewed here); the paper does not evaluate an adaptive/watermark-aware adversary who knows the clustering scheme f and specifically targets cluster boundaries, only generic paraphrasing/substitution tools; and there is a numeric discrepancy between DIPPER TPR/AUROC values stated in the Section 4.2 prose and those listed in Table 2 for the same experimental conditions (both are reported in the facts fields below, flagged rather than silently corrected).

datasets_used: C4 realnewslike (Raffel et al. 2020) -- primary evaluation corpus
ELI5 -- used only for a generalization check reported in Appendix A, not reviewed in full text extracted here
quantitative_results: Clean text, Llama-2-13B: PASA ROC-AUC 1.0000, TPR@1%FPR 1.0000, TPR@10%FPR 1.0000
T5-Large attack, Llama-2-13B: PASA ROC-AUC 0.9946, TPR@1%FPR 0.9296, TPR@10%FPR 0.9899 vs KGW ROC-AUC 0.9750/TPR@1%FPR 0.7350 and DAWA ROC-AUC 0.8067/TPR@1%FPR 0.3300
T5-XXL attack, Llama-2-13B: PASA ROC-AUC 0.9392, TPR@1%FPR 0.8040 vs KGW ROC-AUC 0.9196/TPR@1%FPR 0.6800, Exp-edit ROC-AUC 0.9536/TPR@1%FPR 0.8700
T5-Large attack, Mixtral-8x7B: PASA ROC-AUC 0.9902, TPR@1%FPR 0.9300 vs Exp-edit ROC-AUC 0.9760/TPR@1%FPR 0.9250, AWTI ROC-AUC 0.9972/TPR@1%FPR 0.9400, DAWA ROC-AUC 0.8696/TPR@1%FPR 0.5350
DIPPER paraphrase Ord=0 (Table 2 values): PASA ROC-AUC 0.9132, TPR@1%FPR 0.5900, TPR@10%FPR 0.7970 vs KGW ROC-AUC 0.9032/TPR@1%FPR 0.4750, DAWA ROC-AUC 0.4551/TPR@1%FPR 0.0450
DIPPER paraphrase Ord=80 (Table 2 values): PASA ROC-AUC 0.8956, TPR@1%FPR 0.5377, TPR@10%FPR 0.7593 vs KGW TPR@1%FPR 0.3100, DAWA TPR@1%FPR 0.0200, Exp-edit TPR@1%FPR 0.1150, AWTI TPR@1%FPR 0.1350

Section 4.2 prose reports slightly different DIPPER numbers for the same Ord=0/20/80 settings than Table 2: TPR@1%FPR 0.5578/0.5829/0.5879 and ROC-AUC 0.8776/0.9116/0.8934 -- flagged as an internal text-vs-table inconsistency in the source PDF
Text quality (Table 3): PPL Human 10.41, Unwatermarked 12.41, KGW 11.81, PASA (Ours) 11.44, DAWA 8.41 (lowest), Exp-edit 23.40, AWTI 19.77

Latency (Table 3, per 300-token sample, 200 sequences): generation time Unwatermarked 12.93s, PASA 13.35s, DAWA 13.56s, AWTI 24.24s; detection time KGW 0.04s, PASA 0.27s, DAWA 0.31s, Exp-edit 2.41s, AWTI 10.52s

Ablation on cluster count K: robustness against T5-based token-replacement attacks well preserved for K in [3,100]; degrades rapidly once K exceeds 500; K=4 empirically best overall and used as default
Ablation on window size w under r=0.5 token replacement: TPR@1%FPR falls from 0.7236 (w=3) to 0.1508 (w=8)
